items.q: Roughly what proportion of energy passes from one trophic level to the next?
items.why: Students assume energy is conserved across levels. Most energy is lost as heat (respiration), through movement, indigestible parts, and uneaten material. The 10% rule explains why food chains are short.

items.q: Why are top predators most affected by persistent pollutants like mercury?
items.correct: Biomagnification concentrates pollutants at each trophic level up the food chain.
items.misconception: Predators are exposed to more pollution directly.
items.why: Students focus on direct exposure. The mechanism is concentration through eating contaminated prey - small accumulations in lower levels multiply upward, so apex predators have the highest body burdens.

items.q: What is the leading driver of global deforestation?
items.correct: Agriculture (especially cattle ranching, soy, palm oil).
items.why: Students think trees are felled for wood. While logging contributes, the largest driver is land conversion for agriculture - food production drives most tropical forest loss, especially commodities like beef and palm oil.

items.q: Schaefer's maximum sustainable yield model dates from:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1954. “1935” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Newfoundland cod fishery moratorium was imposed in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1992. “1972” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximate share of consumed fish from aquaculture by 2020 (%)?
items.why: The real figure is 50 %. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
